case_name: Lake water pesticide treatment for drinking supply under moderate budget and limited operator skill
task_description: Recommend a ranked list of feasible treatment process chains for lake water containing tebuconazole and nitrofen intended for drinking water production while balancing contaminant removal, cost, operator simplicity, and residuals handling.

input_data.scenario_background: A small regional utility draws from a shallow lake affected by seasonal agricultural runoff from orchards and former herbicide-use areas. Monitoring shows low-µg/L detections of tebuconazole and nitrofen, with moderate natural organic matter and seasonal algae episodes that can interfere with adsorptive treatment. The utility has a moderate capital budget but limited operator skill and prefers a process that can reliably meet drinking-water expectations without highly specialized membrane or advanced oxidation operation. The key trade-off is that several technologies can remove these hydrophobic pesticides, but the best option must also tolerate variable lake-water quality and avoid excessive operational burden or difficult residuals management.

treatment_goal.target_requirement: Finished water should achieve reliable removal of tebuconazole and nitrofen to levels consistent with protective drinking-water production and maintain stable treated-water quality through seasonal source changes.
secondary_goals: control turbidity and natural organic matter sufficiently to support pesticide removal
limit operator attention and process tuning demands
avoid treatment options with highly difficult concentrate handling
maintain resilience during algae and runoff-driven water quality swings

engineering_constraints.residuals_management: standard solids and spent media handling are acceptable, but avoid high-difficulty liquid concentrate management or highly specialized chemical regimes
engineering_constraints.robustness_requirement: process should remain reliable under seasonal turbidity, NOM, and algae fluctuations typical of shallow lake water

core_unit_functions.coagulation_flocculation_sedimentation: remove suspended solids, algae, and part of natural organic matter to stabilize downstream adsorption performance
core_unit_functions.dual_media_filtration: polish particulates and protect adsorptive media
core_unit_functions.granular_activated_carbon: adsorb hydrophobic micropollutants including tebuconazole and nitrofen
core_unit_functions.disinfection: provide final microbial protection for potable supply

applicability_rationale: both target pesticides are hydrophobic organic micropollutants for which activated carbon is a well-established control approach
upstream clarification and filtration are important because moderate DOC and seasonal algae in lake water would otherwise compete for GAC adsorption sites and shorten bed life
this train balances strong technical feasibility with relatively familiar operation for conventional surface-water plants
it avoids high-pressure membrane concentrate management while still providing a robust multi-barrier configuration for drinking water production
GAC can be implemented as post-filtration contactors with manageable operator burden compared with advanced oxidation or membrane systems
potential_risks: GAC replacement or reactivation frequency may increase if NOM spikes are not well controlled
breakthrough monitoring is needed because pesticide adsorption capacity can decline seasonally
clarifier sludge and spent carbon management still require routine planning

core_unit_functions.coagulation_flocculation_sedimentation: remove solids and part of NOM while capturing PAC-associated solids after contact
core_unit_functions.dual_media_filtration: polish carryover solids and improve finished water clarity
core_unit_functions.powdered_activated_carbon_dosing: provide flexible adsorption of episodic pesticide peaks

applicability_rationale: PAC is a plausible pesticide-control route for surface waters, especially where contamination is seasonal or episodic
the utility can dose PAC more intensively during runoff events instead of maintaining large GAC vessels at all times
this approach can reduce capital burden relative to dedicated GAC contactors if pesticide occurrence is intermittent
the process remains relatively compatible with conventional surface-water treatment operations
potential_risks: performance is more dose-sensitive and depends on contact time, mixing, and source-water competition from NOM
routine optimization may exceed the comfort level of minimally staffed operators
sludge production increases and PAC handling creates housekeeping and dust-control needs

core_unit_functions.coagulation_flocculation_sedimentation: reduce solids and lower oxidant demand before ozonation
core_unit_functions.ozonation: oxidize a portion of oxidizable organic micropollutants and improve downstream biodegradability
core_unit_functions.biological_activated_carbon: adsorb residual pesticides and biologically polish biodegradable ozonation byproducts

applicability_rationale: ozone plus BAC can provide a broad micropollutant barrier and may outperform carbon alone for some mixed organic contaminant situations
the combined process also helps address variable lake-water quality and can improve downstream biologically stable water
it is technically attractive when utilities want broader treatment of multiple organics beyond the two named pesticides
potential_risks: operator skill requirements are higher because ozone dose control and BAC management are more complex
bromate formation risk must be assessed if bromide is present, even at modest levels
process optimization is more demanding than conventional clarification plus GAC

core_unit_functions.enhanced_coagulation: reduce fouling load and NOM ahead of membranes
core_unit_functions.ultrafiltration: provide particulate and algal barrier to protect tighter membranes
core_unit_functions.nanofiltration_or_low_pressure_reverse_o: reject dissolved organic micropollutants including pesticides
core_unit_functions.stabilization: adjust finished water chemistry after membrane treatment

applicability_rationale: NF or RO is technically capable of delivering strong pesticide rejection and very consistent effluent quality
this route is attractive if the utility anticipates future expansion to broader dissolved contaminant control
membranes can handle multiple contaminant targets in a single barrier when well designed and operated
potential_risks: concentrate management is difficult and conflicts with the stated residuals preference
membrane fouling control, integrity monitoring, and chemical cleaning increase operator burden
post-treatment stabilization is necessary and adds complexity

core_unit_functions.coagulation_flocculation_sedimentation: remove solids and reduce UV scavenging load before oxidation
core_unit_functions.dual_media_filtration: polish particulates and improve UV transmittance stability
core_unit_functions.advanced_oxidation_process_uv_h2o2: generate hydroxyl radicals for oxidation of susceptible organic micropollutants

applicability_rationale: AOP is included as a technically plausible route because it can degrade some organic micropollutants rather than transfer them to a solid phase
it may be considered where utilities prefer contaminant destruction over adsorption and where source-water transmittance is favorable
potential_risks: moderate DOC and UV254 in this lake water will increase radical scavenging and reduce AOP efficiency unless doses are increased
compound-specific degradation performance for tebuconazole and nitrofen may be less predictable than adsorption-based removal in full-scale practice
chemical handling, UV system maintenance, and process verification are demanding for a limited-skill operation
